title: Ingestion: From Raw Source to Clean Text
concept: Pulling documents from files, APIs, or databases and normalizing them into clean, parseable text.

exercises.prompt: Why do we build indexing pipelines to connect LLMs to external databases and documents?

options.text: They have unlimited built-in memory for private data
They need a way to access external, updated, or private information
They cannot read English text without a search engine

exercises.explain: Since this is the first lesson of a new unit on Indexing Pipelines, we start with a foundational question about why search prep matters for LLMs. LLMs need external documents because they cannot memorize all private data.

cards.title: The Pipeline Entrance
cards.body: Before an LLM can search through your company's PDFs, emails, or databases, that data has to get inside the system. But LLMs can't read raw files directly—they need clean, organized text to make sense of your world.

cards.title: What is Ingestion?
cards.body: Ingestion is the first step of an LLM indexing pipeline. It involves pulling raw data from various sources (like PDF files, API endpoints, or database tables) and stripping away formatting, metadata, and code to leave behind clean, standardized plain text.

cards.title: The Recycling Sorting Line
cards.body: Think of ingestion like recycling plastic. You collect raw, dirty bottles of all shapes (PDFs, APIs, databases), strip off the sticky labels and metal caps, and melt them down into clean, uniform raw plastic pellets that are ready to be molded.

cards.title: From HTML to Plain Text
cards.body:
If you pull an article from a web API, it might arrive full of HTML tags like '

Hello World

'. The ingestion process strips those tags away, leaving behind the clean, raw string: 'Hello World'.

cards.title: Myth: LLMs Read Raw Files
cards.body: You might think LLMs can naturally digest PDFs, word documents, or database tables directly. In reality, LLMs only read raw strings, and messy formatting or markup tags will confuse the model and waste processing power.

cards.title: Ingestion Transformation
cards.body: Compare the state of document data before and after the ingestion pipeline does its work.

left.text: PDF binaries, raw HTML tags, and nested database tables.

right.text: Standardized, plain text strings free of markup and metadata.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of the Ingestion step in an indexing pipeline?

options.text: To translate documents into multiple languages instantly
To convert messy, multi-format source files into clean, uniform text
To generate vector representations of sentences
To execute code snippets found inside document files

exercises.explain: Raw source documents come in different shapes and layouts; ingestion converts them all into a standard, clean text format.

exercises.prompt: True or False: LLMs can natively read and understand raw PDF binary data without any preprocessing or extraction step.
exercises.explain: LLMs cannot directly process raw binary PDF streams or nested database tables; they require the text to be extracted and cleaned first.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step of the document preparation process:
exercises.sentence: Before sending text to an LLM, we apply normalization to remove messy HTML tags and clean up spacing.
exercises.explain: Normalization is the phase where formatting, tags, and extra spacing are cleaned up to create a standardized plain text format.

exercises.prompt: An AI system was tasked with extracting and cleaning text from a PDF document. Which option shows signs of being an AI-generated template instead of natural, cleaned human text?

options.text: Welcome to the Q3 financial report. Our revenue increased by 12% due to cloud growth.
[DOCUMENT START] [SOURCE: FILE_0921.PDF] PAGE 1 OF 32. CONTENTS: {header_placeholder} Annual financial report statistics. [DOCUMENT END]
Please review the updated policy guidelines regarding remote work equipment reimbursements.

exercises.explain: Option B contains telltale AI artifacts: placeholder variables, a generic summary signature, and overly clinical markup notes rather than raw extracted text.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a document indexing pipeline for a company's internal wiki (HTML format) and policy manuals (PDF format). What is the very first operational step your ingestion pipeline must execute before sending content to a vector database?

options.text: Convert the text into high-dimensional vector embeddings immediately
Pass the raw PDF binary stream directly to the LLM without parsing
Extract raw text from the PDF, strip HTML/Markdown tags, and normalize whitespaces
Store the file metadata in a vector database without saving the actual text

exercises.explain: Before documents can be embedded or split, they must be converted into clean, uniform text. Stripping layout code (like HTML tags) and extracting raw text from binary formats (like PDFs) is the foundation of the ingestion pipeline.
